transcript: ask you is how many times have you snitched on a fellow inmate? This makes my fourth. This makes your fourth time. Four times you've testified for the prosecution against a fellow inmate. Makes you a popular man, Mr. Corliss. Sounds like people just come up to you, tell you the crimes that they've committed so you can come in here and testify again.

speakers.name: Speaker1
speakers.voice_reasoning: The voice is clear, articulate, and authoritative with a hint of condescension, fitting perfectly for a seasoned lawyer systematically dismantling a witness on the stand.
speakers.style_direction: A sharp, confident prosecutor cross-examining a jailhouse informant. His tone is methodical and laced with increasing sarcasm, intended to expose the witness's lack of credibility to the jury.

speakers.name: Speaker2
speakers.voice_reasoning: The speaker's voice is low, slightly gravelly, and delivered in a clipped, reluctant manner, characteristic of a weary inmate who has been through this process before.
speakers.style_direction: A hardened jailhouse informant on the witness stand. He's weary and guarded, answering questions with a low, gruff, and minimal tone.

tagged_transcript:
Speaker1: [like a lawyer asking a pointed question in cross-examination] Toh batao, kitni baar apne hi inmate par snitch kiya hai?
Speaker2: [gruffly and reluctantly] Yeh chauthi baar hai.
Speaker1: [like a lawyer pressing a point in cross-examination] Chauthi baar. Chaar baar tumne prosecution ke liye testify kiya hai apne hi inmate ke against.
Speaker1: [like a lawyer pressing a point, with growing sarcasm] Kaafi popular ho tum, Mr. Corliss. Lagta hai log khud tumhare paas aate hain apne crimes batane... taaki tum yahan testify kar sako.
original_tagged_transcript:
Speaker1: [like a lawyer asking a pointed question in cross-examination] I ask you is how many times have you snitched on a fellow inmate?
Speaker2: [gruffly and reluctantly] This makes my fourth.
Speaker1: [like a lawyer pressing a point in cross-examination] This makes your fourth time. [medium pause] Four times you've testified for the prosecution [short pause] against a fellow inmate.
Speaker1: [like a lawyer pressing a point, with growing sarcasm] Makes you a popular man, Mr. Corliss. [medium pause] Sounds like people just come up to you, tell you the crimes that they've committed so you can come in here and testify again.
